messages.content: The test revealed that the anodic peak current of DAN was increased from pH 2.0 to pH 7.0 and then decreased. The maximum value of pH was observed at pH 7.0.
This suggests that DAN has the highest electrochemical activity at pH 7.0 and its anodic peak current decreases at both lower and higher pH values.

لطفا جمله زیر را بازیابی کنید به انگلیسی. The unmodified CPE (voltammogram a) showed the high anodic (Epa) and cathodic (Epc) peak potentials and lower anodic (Ipa) and cathodic (Ipc) peak currentd, due to the sluggish electron transfer towards DAN.

The unmodified CPE (voltammogram a) exhibited high anodic (Epa) and cathodic (Epc) peak potentials and lower anodic (Ipa) and cathodic (Ipc) peak currents, indicating sluggish electron transfer towards DAN.

لطفا پاراگراف زیر را بازیابی کنید به انگلیسی.The impact of scan rate (υ) on the redox reaction of 1000.0 µM DAN in pH = 7.0 PBS (0.1 M) at MOF-235/GO/CPE was investigated (Figure 5). The Ipa and Ipc increased when scan rates rose from 5 to 100 mV s−1, according to analysis of DAN by CV (Figure 5).

The effect of scan rate (υ) on the redox reaction of 1000.0 µM DAN in pH = 7.0 PBS (0.1 M) at MOF-235/GO/CPE was studied (Figure 5). Analysis of DAN by CV revealed that the anodic peak current (Ipa) and cathodic peak current (Ipc) increased as the scan rate increased from 5 to 100 mV s−1 (Figure 5).

لطفا پاراگراف زیر را بازیابی کنید به انگلیسی. Alternatively, the findings demonstrated that both peak currents (Ipa & Ipc) increased linearly with the square root of υ (υ1/2) for the drug DAN (Figure 5 (Inset)), confirming the explored range of potential scan rates and the diffusion-controlled process for redox reaction of DAN on the MOF-235/GO/CPE.

Alternatively, the results indicated that both peak currents (Ipa & Ipc) exhibited a linear increase with the square root of υ (υ1/2) for the DAN drug (Figure 5, Inset). This confirms the investigated range of potential scan rates and suggests a diffusion-controlled process for the redox reaction of DAN on the MOF-235/GO/CPE.

لطفا پاراگراف زیر را بازیابی کنید به انگلیسی. Adjusting the potential of the working electrode at 0.45 V at a surface of MOF-235/GO/CPE in PBS at a pH value of 7.0 allowed researchers to study the chronoamperometric behaviors of different concentrations of DAN (Figure 6).
By setting the working electrode potential at 0.45 V on the MOF-235/GO/CPE surface in pH 7.0 PBS, the chronoamperometric behaviors of various concentrations of DAN were investigated (Figure 6).

The Cottrell equation describes the electrochemical reaction's current (at a mass transport restricted rate) for an electroactive substance (DAN in this case) with a diffusion coefficient of D:
I=nFAD1/2Cbπ-1/2t-1/2
D stands for diffusion coefficient (cm2 s-1) and Cb for applied bulk concentration (mol cm-3) in the Cottrell equation.

Yes, that's correct. The Cottrell equation describes the current (I) of an electrochemical reaction, which is limited by mass transport, for an electroactive substance like DAN. In the equation, n represents the number of electrons transferred in the reaction, F is the Faraday constant, A is the electrode area, D represents the diffusion coefficient (in cm2 s-1), Cb is the bulk concentration (in mol cm-3) of the substance, and t is the time (in s). The equation is given as:
I = nFAD1/2Cbπ-1/2t-1/2
This equation helps to analyze the electrochemical behavior of DAN and understand its diffusion-controlled process.

I vs. t-1/2 experimental findings have been presented in Figure 6A, with the ideal matches for different DAN concentrations. The electrocatalytic current's linearity with respect to t-1/2 suggests that DAN must diffuse from the majority of the solution to the surface of the electrode in order to control the current. Following that, the slopes that corresponded to the lines in Figure 6A that have been drawn straight were plotted against the DAN concentration (Figure 6B). The resulting slope and the Cottrell equation were used to calculate the mean value of D, which has been discovered to be 1.4 ×10-6cm2/s.

The experimental data presented in Figure 6A showed a linear relationship between I (current) and t-1/2 (time to the power of -1/2) for different concentrations of DAN. This linearity suggests that the current is controlled by the diffusion of DAN from the bulk solution to the electrode surface. The slopes of the lines in Figure 6A, which represent the electrocatalytic current, were plotted against the corresponding DAN concentrations in Figure 6B. By using the slope values and the Cottrell equation, the mean value of D (diffusion coefficient) was calculated to be 1.4 ×10-6 cm2/s.

Chronoamperograms obtained at MOF–235/GO/CPE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) for various concentration of DAN (chronoamperograms a to e related to 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 mM of DAN). Inset A: the linear dependence between Ip (µA) vs t-1/2 (s-1/2) and Inset B: linear dependence between slope values of I-t-1/2 plots vs DAN concentrations.

The chronoamperograms acquired at MOF-235/GO/CPE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) for different concentrations of DAN (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 mM) were analyzed. The inset A of the figure shows a linear relationship between the peak current (Ip, in µA) and the square root of time (t-1/2, in s-1/2), indicating diffusion-controlled behavior. Inset B depicts a linear dependence between the slope values obtained from the I-t-1/2 plots and the concentrations of DAN.

Using DPV at MOF-235/GO/CPE, it has been determined whether the prepared sensor could be used for a quantitative analysis of DAN by looking at the correlation of the Ipa and the concentration of DAN (Figure 7). The calibration curve (Figure 7 (Inset)) under optimized circumstances displayed a linear range from 0.005 to 400.0 μM with equations Ipa (µA) = 0.0152 CDAN (μM) + 0.9772 (R2 = 0.995). The increase in DAN concentration from 0.005 to 400.0 μM results in a linear increase in the peak current. Using the calibration plot, a detection limit of 0.002 μM (based on S/N = 3) was calculated for DAN.

The suitability of the MOF-235/GO/CPE sensor for quantitative analysis of DAN was investigated using Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV). The correlation between the anodic peak current (Ipa) and the concentration of DAN was examined (Figure 7). Under optimized conditions, the calibration curve (Inset, Figure 7) exhibited a linear range of 0.005 to 400.0 μM, with the equation Ipa (µA) = 0.0152 CDAN (μM) + 0.9772 and a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.995. As the concentration of DAN increased from 0.005 to 400.0 μM, the peak current displayed a linear increase. Through the calibration plot, a detection limit of 0.002 μM (based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3) was determined for DAN.

DPVs of MOF–235/GO/CPE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing various concentrations of DAN (DPVs a to i correspond to 0.005, 0.1, 5.0, 30.0, 70.0, 100.0, 200.0, 300.0, and 400.0 μM of DAN respectively). Inset: plots of Ipa vs. DAN concentrations.

Differential Pulse Voltammograms (DPVs) were obtained for MOF-235/GO/CPE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) with different concentrations of DAN. The DPV curves (a to i) correspond to concentrations of 0.005, 0.1, 5.0, 30.0, 70.0, 100.0, 200.0, 300.0, and 400.0 μM of DAN, respectively. The inset of the figure displays the plots of the anodic peak current (Ipa) as a function of the DAN concentrations.

The good activity of MOF-235/GO/CPE permits the DPV method's simultaneous detection of DAN and DTIC (Figure 8). Using MOF-235/GO/CPE, two distinct anodic peaks associated with the electrooxidation of DAN and DTIC were seen on DPV curves. Furthermore, two DAN and DTIC peaks at 390 V and 750 V, respectively, were identified in the DPV responses. The outcomes show that the two medicines can be distinguished well in combination solutions.

The excellent performance of MOF-235/GO/CPE enables the simultaneous detection of DAN and DTIC using the DPV method (Figure 8). With the utilization of MOF-235/GO/CPE, distinct anodic peaks corresponding to the electrooxidation of DAN and DTIC were observed in the DPV curves. Additionally, two separate peaks for DAN and DTIC were identified at 390 V and 750 V, respectively, in the DPV responses. These results indicate that the two drugs can be effectively distinguished in mixed solutions using this approach.

The Ipa of DAN and Ipa of DTIC increased linearly with concentration at the concentration region  (Figure 8A, 8B). As shown, two distinct peaks with ∆E ~ 360 mV were found at a surface of MOF-235/GO/CPE for DAN and DTIC, which is sufficient for the simultaneous study of two anticancer drugs. Additionally, levels of sensitivity to DAN both when DTIC is present and absent have been discovered to be 0.0152 µA/µM (in the absence of DTIC) and 0.0153 µA/µM (in the presence of DTIC). As a result, utilizing the modified electrode in matrix samples allows for the determination of these compounds at the same time.

The anodic peak current (Ipa) of DAN and DTIC exhibited a linear increase with concentration in the respective concentration range (Figure 8A, 8B). It was observed that two distinct peaks with a potential separation (∆E) of approximately 360 mV were present on the surface of MOF-235/GO/CPE for DAN and DTIC, which is suitable for the simultaneous analysis of these two anticancer drugs. Furthermore, the sensitivity levels for DAN in the absence and presence of DTIC were found to be 0.0152 µA/µM and 0.0153 µA/µM, respectively. Hence, using the modified electrode approach in matrix samples facilitates the simultaneous determination of these compounds.

The determination of DAN and DTIC in real samples were also investigated on MOF-235/GO/CPE to confirm practicability.  The results of this investigation, which used the standard addition method, are shown in Table 1. The obtained results indicated that the recoveries of these compounds are acceptable and the MOF-235/GO/CPE can potentially be used to determine DAN and DTIC drugs in real samples.

To assess the practicality of MOF-235/GO/CPE, the determination of DAN and DTIC in real samples was also examined. The standard addition method was employed, and the results of this investigation are presented in Table 1. The obtained results demonstrated that the recoveries of these compounds were within an acceptable range, suggesting that MOF-235/GO/CPE has the potential to be utilized for the determination of DAN and DTIC drugs in real samples.
